ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nanotechnology has revolutionized the biomedical frontier by offering innovative diagnostic and therapeutic solutions to existing or emerging diseases. However, the physicochemical synthesis of nanostructures demands an eco-friendly, cost-effective, and sustainable way of nanomaterial synthesis. Plant biomass-derived nanomaterials are more biocompatible, eco-friendly and promote sustainability as these harness the natural capabilities of plants to act as reducing and stabilizing agents.

This review prospects different plant biomass-based nanomaterials synthesized utilizing plant-derived precursors to minimize environmental impact. In biosensing, these nanostructures have proven their value and exhibited high performance equivalent to the physiochemically synthesized nanomaterials for health and environmental monitoring. Their crucial role in therapeutic applications and diverse bioimaging modalities like fluorescence, magnetic resonance, photoacoustic, and computed tomography have further endorsed their potential. Integration into theragnostics and multimodal imaging reveals fresh avenues for personalized medicine. This review provides a comprehensive summary of recent advancements of plant-derived nanomaterials for diagnostics and therapeutics. It also discusses the associated challenges of scalability, reproducibility, and stability that acts as a propelling force to conduct further research and unlock the full potential of these nanomaterials.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Plant biomass-derived nanomaterials are synthesized using diverse plant components like leaves, roots, flowers, stems, seeds, fruit peels or their extracts to convert metal ions into nanoparticles (NPs), as illustrated in Fig. 1 (Noah and Ndangili, 2022). The plant biomass or its extracts possess bioactive substances such as proteins, alkaloids, flavonoids, terpenoids, and phenolics that function as stabilizing and reducing agents (Singh et al., 2023). Plant-based methods of NPs synthesis are referred to as the "green synthesis" approach, as the resulting nanomaterials are environmentally benign. It is a sustainable and eco-friendly alternative to the traditional "physical and chemical synthesis processes," which often employ toxic chemicals and involve higher energy input. The availability of plant biomass, simplicity, cost-effectiveness, and its environment-friendly nature facilitate this route toward large-scale synthesis of nanomaterials. These NPs exhibit very interesting optical, electrical, and catalytic characteristics, which render them extremely promising for biosensing and bioimaging purposes (Wang et al., 2024). Their biocompatibility, minimal toxicity, and easy functionalization with biomolecules or ligands enhance their utility in real-time and precise detection of disease markers in physiological conditions (Barathi et al., 2024). These nanomaterials find application in detecting clinically significant molecules like glucose (Dong et al., 2021), cholesterol (Syed et al., 2024), and DNA (Tharani et al., 2022) as well as for detecting environmental pollutants like pesticides, heavy metals, and microbial infections (Oluwaseun Adetunji et al., 2022).

NPs can be utilized in bioimaging as they possess photoacoustic, magnetic, fluorescent or luminescent characteristics. For instance, gold NPs (AuNPs) are employed in optical imaging because of their potent light-scattering properties (Qiao and Qi, 2021). Similarly, quantum dots made from plant biomass provide great options for fluorescence imaging because they provide stability (Kumar et al., 2023). In a similar line, green synthesis-derived iron oxide NPs (IONPs) are used as contrast agents in magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) (Augustine and Hasan, 2020)). Plant-derived capping agents' functional groups offer the opportunity to modify their surfaces, enabling conjugation with therapeutic agents or targeting ligands to improve their administration and imaging specificity in cancer and other diseases (Barathi et al., 2024).

NPs derived from plant biomass are being incorporated into multipurpose theragnostic platforms, which integrate therapeutic and diagnostic functionalities. Their eco-friendly synthesis ensures lower cytotoxicity, which aligns with the requirements of biomedical applications. Apart from following the principles of sustainability, the plant biomass-based synthesis of NPs introduces a number of applications that enhance environmental monitoring and healthcare (Zahra et al., 2023, Gulia et al., 2022). Overall, this article presents the plant biomass-based NPs that provide a sustainable, versatile, and highly effective equivalent for the development of biosensing and bioimaging technologies in the areas of healthcare and the environment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and extraction methods of plant biomass-derived nanomaterials
ce:section.ce:para: Nanomaterials are synthesized via two main approaches: bottom-up and top-down, as depicted in Fig. 2. Bottom-up synthesis synthesizes nanomaterials from discrete atoms or molecules by chemical processes such as hydrothermal, sol-gel, and redox reactions, as well as biological processes involving plant biomass, microbes, and biopolymers (Ijaz et al., 2020). Conversely, the top-down method fragments bulk materials into nanoscale sizes via physical processes like ball milling, lithography, and laser ablation. Each synthesis method possesses distinct advantages and disadvantages, affecting scalability, cost, and the environment. The synthesis approach to be used relies on the desired properties of the nanomaterials and the proposed applications in medicine, energy, and environmental technologies (Abid et al., 2022).

Plant extract-assisted synthesis of nanomaterials is a cost-effective and green technique that utilizes the reducing, stabilizing, and capping capacity of phytochemicals like alkaloids, flavonoids, tannins, and phenolic compounds (Devi et al., 2024). The synthesis process involves combining metal precursors with aqueous plant extracts under controlled temperature, pH, and reaction time. These parameters control the size, shape, and stability of NPs. For example, the right pH and extract concentration can produce NPs with spherical, triangular, or rod shapes. Stability is improved by natural capping agents from the plant extract, providing resistance to aggregation. The process employs renewable natural resources to generate biocompatible nanomaterials, which is in line with sustainable and environmentally friendly practices (Ishak et al., 2019). Different synthesis methods and the characteristics of plant biomass-derived nanomaterials are listed in Table 1 .

The major process of metal NP synthesis from plant biomass involves the reduction of metal ions to their metallic form by phytochemicals. The process consists of three steps: (a) activation and reduction, (b) nucleation, and (c) growth and stabilization. These processes are catalyzed by phytochemicals in plant extracts, which serve as reducing, stabilizing, and capping agents.

(a) Activation and Reduction- Various bioactive molecules in a plant extract come into contact with metal ions upon the addition of a metal salt (such as silver nitrate or gold chloride) to the sample. Flavonoids, phenolic acids, alkaloids, proteins, terpenoids, and polysaccharides are some biomolecules possessing significant reducing ability. Due to the transfer of electrons of these molecules upon contact, the metal ions get reduced. For instance, Au³ ⁺ ions from gold chloride are reduced to Au⁰ and Ag+ ions from silver nitrate are reduced to Ag⁰ to form elemental metal (Bhushan et al., 2024). (b) Nucleation- Nucleation begins as soon as there are sufficient reduced metal atoms in the solution. The mechanism through which single metal atoms aggregate to create stable nuclei that serve as the seeds for NPs development is referred to as nucleation. As the rate of nucleation determines the size and dispersion of the NPs, this process is extremely critical. Although slower nucleation would lead to greater particles due to secondary growth, rapid nucleation yields smaller NPs. (c) Growth and Stabilization- Following nucleation, as more metal atoms with lower reduction remain attached to the nuclei, growth of the NPs occurs. Several factors such as pH, reaction temperature, and concentration of plant extract influence the growth process. Several phytochemicals act as capping agents, stabilizing the size and shape of NPs and preventing uncontrolled aggregation. In this way, for the purpose of dispersion and to prevent agglomeration, these stabilizing molecules adhere on the surface of the nanoparticle and exert steric or electrostatic (El-Seedi et al., 2019).

For example, Kumari et al. (2020) employed aqueous extraction method to biosynthesize AuNPs from Lawsonia inermis leaf extract. The AuNP size, stability, and yield were significantly influenced by the synthesis conditions, including extract concentration (4–8 %) and reaction time. Stability was tested through the use of varied pH, salt, temperature, and storage conditions during the synthesis process. The NPs synthesized were in the range of 10–40 nm, and 4 % concentration of extract yielded the optimal stability of AuNPs. Though the process involved the advantages such as ease of synthesis, biocompatibility, but involved certain drawbacks such as NP aggregation at elevated concentrations of extracts (8 %), which pointed out scalability and environmental adaptability issues, and reduced stability at pH extremes and elevated temperatures (Kumari et al., 2020). In another similar study, Khane et al. (2022) enhanced the synthesis of silver NPs (AgNPs) from Citrus limon zest extract to produce NPs with high stability and purity. The extraction process entailed refluxing the zest in deionized water, then centrifugation, to ensure homogeneity of the nanoparticles. Though the process resulted in stable AgNPs with fine antibacterial and antioxidant activities, a limitation of this research was the possible fluctuation in the size and distribution of nanoparticles depending on experimental conditions, which might influence reproducibility (Khane et al., 2022). In addition to this study, Oves et al. (2022) used Conocarpus lancifolius fruit extract in another study to synthesize AgNPs through a bottom-up, green synthesis method. The synthesis process involved the preparation of a fruit extract by boiling and filtration, followed by silver nitrate incubation for the synthesis of nanoparticles. The size of AgNPs was found to range from 5–30 nm and remained stable for 50 days. Purity and homogeneity were maintained by repeated centrifugation and drying in this synthesis protocol. The maximum yield was temperature- and incubation time-dependent, though stability of the synthesized NPs for long durations was still a concern, which has to be addressed (Oves et al., 2022). Lomelí-Rosales et al. (2022) successfully synthesized NPs (AuNPs and AgNPs) from extracts of Capsicum chinense, obtained from various plant parts (root, stem, leaf), by microwave-assisted synthesis under optimized conditions. With diameters of 10–40 nm, most of the AuNPs were spherical in shape, and the AgNPs were quasi-spherical or spherical with diameters between 15 and 50 nm. The NPs showed excellent stability for up to 15 days, but nanoparticle stabilization varied with different volumes of plant extracts. The limitation of the present study was incomplete synthesis of the NPs from some plant extracts. It was noticed that although the extracts of other plants were able to synthesize the NPs with success, when Ginkgo biloba was used, there was incomplete or partial nanoparticle formation due to less efficiency of the reaction (Lomelí-Rosales et al., 2022).

Metal oxide nanoparticle synthesis usually necessitates further oxidation stages to generate metal-oxygen linkages, whereas metal nanoparticle synthesis utilizes the reduction of metal ions by phytochemicals. Functionalized amino groups (-NH₂) in proteins are essential for the reduction of metal ions to metal oxide NPs. Furthermore, alkaloids, phenols, anthracenes, and flavones have functional groups like -C–O–C–, -C–O–, -CC–, and -CO– that aid in the synthesis of metal oxide NPs. Flavonoids' many functional groups give them a considerable reducing potential. A significant technique is tautomeric transformation, which releases hydrogen atoms to cause flavonoids to structurally shift from the enol form to the keto form. Their capacity to reduce metal ions is improved by this process, which results in the formation of NPs (Singh et al., 2018). For instance, ZnO NPs synthesized using thyme leaf extract exhibited high purity and enhanced optical qualities (Karam and Abdulrahman, 2022), while Hardwickia binata leaf extract yielded spherical ZnO NPs with various biological applications (Manimegalai et al., 2024). Both studies highlighted the effectiveness of plant extracts as bio-reducing and stabilizing agents, ensuring eco-friendly synthesis. However, limitations such as nanoparticle aggregation due to strong interparticle interactions and insufficient studies on long-term stability affect their uniformity and practical applications. These challenges underscore the need for further optimization to improve nanoparticle dispersion and shelf life, ensuring their efficacy in bioimaging and biomedical applications. Sukumaran et al. (2024) reported the green synthesis of CeO2 NPs using Moringa citrifolia leaf extract as a reducing agent, which resulted in stable and reproducible NPs. The crystalline fluorite cubic structure was confirmed by characterization, and the NPs had spherical and hexagonal structures and a diameter ranging from 29.2 to 45 nm. The NPs exhibited outstanding antibacterial action against both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria. The process ensured a high yield, uniformity, and low impurity content. However, the study did not address the long-term stability of the CeO2 NPs in various environmental conditions (Sukumaran et al., 2024). Buarki et al. (2022) synthesized α-Fe2O3 NPs (NPs) using flower extract from Hibiscus rosa-sinensis. The phytochemicals of the extract served as capping and reducing agents, while iron chloride tetrahydrate used as a precursor. The yield was high while stability was maintained for two weeks using the microwave-assisted extraction method. Significant antibacterial activity was shown by this method against a variety of bacterial strains. But the limitation of this study was polydispersity in nanoparticle shapes and sizes (Buarki et al., 2022).

Metal and metal oxide nanoparticle synthesis involves the formation of individual NPs through reduction and oxidation processes by using phytochemicals, whereas nanocomposite synthesis is the process of combining metal oxide NPs with other nanomaterials. This green synthesis method not only develops NPs but also makes it easier to assemble them into multipurpose composites with enhanced properties for cutting-edge applications.

For instance, the Ag@CNT (silver and carbon nanotube) composite was synthesized by Hefnawy et al. (2023) via a green reduction method using green tea extract as a reducing agent. The green tea extract was obtained by boiling green tea leaves in distilled water, followed by cooling, filtration, and storage in a dark bottle. The silver NPs (Ag) were synthesized by adding the green tea extract to a solution containing silver nitrate and carbon nanotubes (CNT). The size of the silver NPs in the composite (Ag@CNT) was approximately 160 nm, and the particles exhibited a spherical shape. The composite demonstrated good stability, with negligible degradation over extended electrochemical assays. In this method, the purity of the nanocomposite was high, with minimal residual impurities remaining after ethanol washing (Hefnawy et al., 2023). In addition to this study, Sabouri et al. (2024) synthesized Ag-doped ZnO/MgO nanocomposites via a green chemistry method using Caccinia macranthera extract as a reducing and stabilizing agent. After two hours of heating the plant material, the extract was obtained by filtering it. In order to synthesize Ag-doped ZnO/MgO nanocomposites, the extract was mixed with aqueous solutions of zinc nitrate, magnesium nitrate, and silver nitrate under meticulously controlled conditions. The NPs exhibited notable photocatalytic activity for MB dye degradation and efficient Pb²⁺ ion sensing with a low detection limit (LOD). However, scalability and long-term stability under industrial conditions remain a challenge (Sabouri et al., 2024). The Fe₂O₃/ZnO nanocomposites, synthesized by Ameen et al. (2023) using Thymus vulgaris extract, exhibited high purity. The nanocomposites demonstrated good stability, maintaining 78 % of their catalytic performance after four reuse cycles in hydrogen production studies. Their consistent photodegradation efficiency against MB (Methylene blue) dye under sunlight further highlights their robust stability. These findings underline the nanocomposites' suitability for environmental and catalytic applications. However, the multi-day synthesis process and scalability limitations may hinder large-scale production (Ameen et al., 2023). Apart from this, the NiO NPs and Cu–NiO nanoclusters were synthesized using a green extraction method with Phytolacca dodecandra leaf extract by Firisa et al. (2022). The NPs exhibited a spherical morphology with an average size of approximately 20–40 nm. However, the study had limitations such as batch-dependent yield and reproducibility, influenced by variations in the phytochemical composition of the plant extract (Firisa et al., 2022).

Carbon-based nanomaterial synthesis from plant biomass is distinct from metal or metal oxide nanoparticle synthesis since it relies upon carbonization of organic carbon instead of metal ion reduction or oxidation. Plant biomass is a rich carbon source and upon pyrolysis or hydrothermal treatment, forms nanostructures. For example, CDs were prepared from leaves of Azadirachta indica (neem) through a green sand bath method by Gedda et al. (2023). Synthesis included refluxing of neem leaf extract, centrifugation, and filtration to purify CDs, which showed high stability under UV light and long-term storage. CDs showed homogeneity with a particle size of ∼3.5 nm (TEM) and ∼34.02 nm (DLS). This green method ensured non-toxic, biocompatible CDs for biomedical applications, but the drawback was the lack of complete understanding of the molecular transformation mechanism (synthesis process) (Gedda et al., 2023). Besides this work, activated carbon derived from Moringa oleifera fruit shells and multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) were used as supercapacitor electrode materials. ZnCl₂ was employed to activate and carbonize the fruit shells at various temperatures to achieve maximum porosity. Activated carbon was further mixed with N-methylpyrrolidone, polyvinylidene fluoride, and MWCNTs to create electrodes. Even displaying high capacitance and energy density, the study was only applicable for lab-scale production of these nanomaterials (Palisoc et al., 2020). In addition to this study, pyrolysis process was employed for the production of activated carbon and multi-walled carbon nanotubes from Miscanthus × giganteus biomass through chemical activation by Osman et al. (2020). The biomass was first activated with H₃PO₄ and KOH, followed by blending with melamine (nitrogen source) and iron oxalate (catalyst). Pyrolysis resulted in MWCNT synthesis with increased surface area and hydrophilicity. The process was efficient in terms of nanotube growth with enhanced structural and textural features. The hydrophilicity of the CNTs was making them favorable for use in wastewater treatment and microelectronics. Yet, limitations lied in the possible impurities present in activated carbon, decreased surface area in CNTs owing to amorphous carbon, and additional optimization of the synthesis process required for large-scale usage (Osman et al., 2020). Aside from the present work, fluorescent CDs was prepared using pre-extraction wastes of lignocellulosic biomass (switchgrass, pine, and yellow poplar) through hydrothermal carbonization at 220°C for 5 hours. The plant raw materials were subject to hot-water pre-extraction at 120–180°C for 30–60 minutes. The CDs had tuneable quantum yields (6.2 %–11.8 %) and were utilized in temperature sensing, anti-counterfeiting, and bioimaging. However, the limitation of this study was requiring accurate control of extraction conditions in order to optimize yield and fluorescence characteristics, as well as scaling the method up for industrial-scale applications was still unexplored (Zhang et al., 2020).

Natural nanomaterials derived from plant biomass, such as cellulose and lignin, are sourced from renewable plant materials and exhibit unique structural properties due to their natural origin. The extraction of cellulose or lignin-based nanomaterials uses comparatively easy, environmentally friendly methods like mechanical grinding, acid hydrolysis, or enzymatic processes, which makes them more sustainable and biodegradable for a variety of applications than other nanomaterials that frequently require synthetic chemicals or high-energy processes for synthesis. For instance, the cellulose nanocrystals (CNCs) were isolated from peanut shells by Terea et al. (2024). A multi-step process involving washing, chemical treatment using sodium hydroxide (NaOH) and hydrochloric acid (HCl), and bleaching to remove impurities was used to isolate the CNCs. Isolation of CNCs was done by hydrolyzing sulfuric acid. The CNCs with 77.95 % crystallinity index were assessed for purity and crystallinity. After loading them onto CNCs, zinc oxide NPs (ZnO NPs) produced CNC/ZnO NPs of different sizes and crystal structures. Depending on the synthesis method, the CNC/ZnO NPs were hexagonal in shape and varied from 20.14 to 27.31 nm. The synthesis and stability of CNC/ZnO NPs were optimized for uniformity. The method created a potential composite for antibacterial application by showing high purity and effective nanoparticle embedding. Nonetheless, a restriction of the synthesis strategy was the agglomeration of ZnO NPs on CNCs, which could influence their stability and homogeneity (Terea et al., 2024). Shafiee et al. (2024) reported lignin NPs (Lignin-NPs) derived from almond peel, synthesized using a hydrothermal method. Almond peel powder was combined with distilled water and heated under controlled conditions to create the NPs, which had a spherical morphology and a size of about 31 nm as determined by TEM. The NPs were well-dispersed, with an average size of about 350 nm in solution, and the yield was maximized by freeze-drying (Shafiee et al., 2024).

Green synthesis of NPs from plant biomass extracts has numerous benefits that make them an excellent alternative to traditional techniques. Based on green chemistry principles, the technique is eco-friendly because it eliminates the use of hazardous chemicals and a lot of energy inputs. The use of plant extracts streamlines the process of synthesis and reduces costs by serving as natural stabilizing and reducing agents. In addition, the method is versatile, such that a vast range of plants and NPs with different unique properties can be synthesized (Bhushan et al., 2024). Nevertheless, there are drawbacks, such as variations in extract composition based on various plant species, cultivation environments, and extraction techniques, which may impact the uniformity of NPs. Furthermore, the comparatively slower reaction times and possible difficulties in attaining exact control over the size and structure of NPs continue to be major challenges. Optimizing the synthesis procedure and achieving the full potential of plant-derived NPs in a range of applications require addressing these constraints (Gupta et al., 2023).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biosensing application of plant biomass-derived nanomaterial
ce:section.ce:para: Plant extract-mediated synthesized nanomaterials have received great interest in biosensing for their environmentally friendly synthesis and peculiar properties (Pechyen et al., 2024). Nanomaterials enhance biosensor performance dramatically by increasing sensitivity, selectivity, and biocompatibility, allowing for the detection of biomolecules, pathogens, and environmental contaminants. Their green and low-cost materials provide potential routes to achieve high-performance biosensing platforms with less environmental impact (Chaudhary et al., 2023). Different nanomaterials derived from plant biomass and biosensing applications are presented in Table 2.

NPs derived from plant biomass have shown significant potential in disease detection through biosensing applications. These NPs enhance sensor sensitivity and specificity, enabling the detection of biomarkers associated with various diseases such as cancer, diabetes, and other infectious conditions. Their excellent biocompatibility and surface functionalization capabilities allow for targeted interactions with biomolecules like proteins, nucleic acids, and antigens. (Karnwal et al., 2024)

For instance, AuNPs synthesized from green tea leaves were utilized to develop a fiber-optic ball tip resonator biosensor for specifically targeting cancer biomarker CD44 by Ashikbayeva et al. (2023). It sensed the change in light intensity due to total internal reflection inside the optical fiber, and the ball resonator device minimized fabrication complexity as well as enhance spectral detection. AuNPs were introduced fiber surface via linkage through 3-aminopropyltrimethoxysilane (APTMS). The CD44 antibody was introduced on the nanoparticle surface through 11-Mercaptoundecanoic acid (MUA) linker and carbodiimide chemistry. The detection mechanism relies on changes in refractive index (RI) due to the binding of the target biomarker, leading to measurable shifts in signal intensity. The biosensors demonstrate high sensitivity (up to 13.17 dB change) and specificity for CD44, with a LOD of 0.111pM. However, the major limitation lies in the interference of cross-reactivity with similar proteins in complex samples (Ashikbayeva et al., 2023). Moringa leaves act as a good reducing and stabilizing agent for the synthesis of NPs. A green method was developed by Basu et al. (2023) to synthesize copper iodide NPs (CuI-NPs) using morin as a stabilizing agent. Fabricated through γ-irradiation, the NPs exhibited enhanced surface activity and a porous structure, enabling effective detection of the cancer biomarker CA 19–9. The detection mechanism relies on the strong interaction between the glycoprotein cancer biomarker carbohydrate antigen 19–9 (CA 19–9) and CuI-NPs. The NPs bind selectively to CA 19–9 through electrostatic and π-system interactions, leading to spectral changes measurable by UV–visible spectroscopy. Enhanced surface activity of γ-irradiated CuI-NPs improved signal sensitivity to 0.066 U/mL in serum with negligible cross-reactivity (Basu et al., 2023). Similarly, the extract of Moringa oleifera leaves was used as a reducing and stabilizing agent to synthesize ZnO NPs. These NPs were immobilized onto a Glassy Carbon Electrode (GCE) to develop an electrochemical biosensor for dopamine detection. Upon interaction with ZnO nanoparticle-modified GCE, dopamine underwent a quasi-reversible redox reaction and got oxidized to dopamine-o-quinone. This redox process generated distinct electrochemical signals due to ZnO NPs mediated enhanced electron transfer offering a vast detection range and LOD of 0.3 μM. Selective interaction with dopamine molecules minimized interference from glucose and ascorbic acid. A limitation of the ZnO nanoparticle-modified GCE was its susceptibility to surface fouling during repeated dopamine measurements, which could affect long-term performance (Manikandan et al., 2024). Kiio et al. (2024) biosynthesized AuNPs using Mangifera indica leaf extract, which acted as a reducing and stabilizing agent due to its rich metabolites. The NPs were used to fabricate a multiplex electrochemical immunosensor for simultaneous detection of CEA and YES1, lung cancer biomarkers ( Fig. 3). Specific antibodies for both biomarkers were immobilized on the gold-modified electrode, and signals were detected by differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) with negligible cross-reactivity to obtain detection limits of 0.0034 and 0.0022 ng/mL for CEA and YES1, respectively. In the presence of the target analyte, electron transfer was altered due to target binding, enhancing the current and enabling biomarker quantification through these signal changes. However, the limitation of this assay was the dependency on pH optimization for sensor accuracy (Kiio et al., 2024). An immunosensor was designed on interdigitated electrodes (IDE) using green-synthesized (IONPs) derived from durian plant extracts by Zhou et al. (2022) for the early detection of rheumatoid arthritis (RA). The IONPs were functionalized with amine-aldehyde linkers, facilitating the immobilization of cyclic citrullinated peptide (CCP) as a probe. The interaction of anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies (anti-CCP) with CCP resulted in a measurable increase in current due to enhanced electron transfer efficiency. A high sensitivity of 8 pg/mL was achieved, with excellent specificity and minimal cross-reactivity. Spiking anti-CCP in human serum confirmed the sensor's robustness however, detection of the target in the presence of a similar molecular structured protein mixture still could be a limitation of this assay (Zhou et al., 2022). In another study, Calendula officinalis extract and quince seed mucilage were used for the synthesis of a composite of gold and silver NPs and for developing a label-free electrochemical immunosensor for prostate-specific antigen (PSA) detection. The mucilage provided a biopolymeric substrate, enhancing nanoparticle assembly and electrochemical efficiency. Antibodies immobilized on nanoparticle modified electrode interacted with PSA, increasing electron transfer resistance measured via electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). The sensor demonstrated a linear detection range from 0.1 pg/mL to 100 ng/mL, with a LOD of 0.078 pg/mL, excellent specificity (<10 % cross-reactivity), and high stability. Although the assay was very specific, the limitation of this immunosensor was the extended incubation time required for optimal detection, particularly for antibody immobilization and PSA-antibody interaction. This increased the overall assay duration, which may limit its applicability in scenarios demanding rapid detection (Darvishi et al., 2021). AgNPs were synthesized using Sclerocarya birrea leaf extract by Alula et al. (2023) to develop a colorimetric sensor for the detection of cysteine. Cysteine, an amino acid, is crucial for metabolic processes and serves as a biomarker for oxidative stress. The oxidation of o-phenylenediamine (OPD) to 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP), a colorful product, was facilitated by the AgNPs' peroxidase-like activity. Because cysteine interacted with the AgNPs to prevent OPD oxidation, the detection technique depended on cysteine's inhibition of this peroxidase-like activity. The absorbance reduced due to this inhibition and was related to the concentration of cysteine. For a range of 0.5–20 μM, the LOD of the system was 90.4 nm, proving high sensitivity. Nevertheless, the existence of large quantities of other thiol-containing substances, such as glutathione, which did not exhibit appreciable inhibitory activity, can influence the detection process. The absorbance was reduced due to this inhibition and was related to the concentration of cysteine. For a linear range of 0.5–20 μM, the LOD of the system was 90.4 nm, proving high sensitivity. Nevertheless, the existence of another thiol-containing chemical, such as glutathione, which did not exhibit an appreciable inhibitory effect, can influence the detection process (Alula, 2023).

Pathogens, including bacteria, viruses, fungi, and parasites, cause diseases in humans, animals, and plants. Green synthesized nanomaterials enable rapid diagnostics that detect pathogen-specific DNA, proteins, or antigens (Verma et al., 2021). For instance, Pebdeni et al. (2020) reported a fluorometric aptasensor based on FRET (Fluorescent Resonance Energy Transfer) for detecting Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus). The nanomaterial employed was CQDS synthesized from olive leaves using the hydrothermal method. The CQDs acted as the donor fluorophores in the FRET process, whereas AuNPs functioned as the acceptors. The detection mechanism of the sensor was through fluorescence quenching of CQDs in the presence of AuNPs, which was reversed upon aptamer binding to S. aureus, releasing AuNPs and CQDs (Fig. 3B). The re-emission of the fluorescence was then detected, providing a signal for the presence of the bacteria, and the LOD was reported as 10 CFU/mL. The sensor showed high specificity because no cross-reactivity was observed with other bacterial strains, such as E. coli, Salmonella typhimurium, or Pseudomonas aeruginosa. Certain limitations are possible interference in real sample matrices, which require further optimization for complex samples.(Pebdeni et al., 2020). Patel et al. (2023) developed a label-free, non-faradaic impedimetric biosensor using microfabricated gold interdigitated electrodes (IDE) for detecting E. coli using neem leaf extract mediated green synthesized AgNPs. The IDEs were biofunctionalized with AgNPs using thiol-gold covalent bonding. The AgNPs functionalized gold interdigitated electrodes trapped E. coli cells, causing membrane disruption and an increase in impedance. The rise in impedance reflected bacterial interaction, indicating cell damage and detection. The absence of cross-reactivity with other microorganisms, such as those often found in water, demonstrated the assay's specificity for E. coli. However, the current study does not mention testing for other bacteria, so additional experiments would be required to establish this specificity (Patel et al., 2023).

Pesticides have wide applications in agriculture to manage pests and boost crop production, but excessive application affects human health and the environment. The detection of pesticides is crucial for ensuring food safety and environmental monitoring(Praharaj and Nara, 2024). Plant-based NPs offer a novel solution by utilizing bioactive molecules from plants to detect pesticides via catalytic or colorimetric processes (Pouthika and Madhumitha, 2024).

Thiram is a common fungicide with extensive environmental impact. Gokulakrishnan et al. (2023) described green synthesis of CDs functionalized AgNPs (Ag/CDs) using the leaf extract of Datura metel. The NPs were utilized in surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) to detect thiram. Whereas upon interaction of the substrate Ag/CDs with thiram molecules, the surface localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) influenced the Raman signal to make it more intensified, and charge transfer between thiram and AgNPs opened the door to chemisorption and increased SERS signal intensity. The detection range of the assay was 10−3 - 10−8 M, which obtained the LOD value of 10−8 M, much lower than its maximum residual limit. But variations in nanoparticle size were seen to potentially affect reproducibility (Gokulakrishnan et al., 2023). Pentachlorophenol (PCP) is a toxic pesticide and is recognized as a persistent organic pollutant. A green and sensitive PCP detecting electrochemical sensor was prepared using nickel ferrite NPs (NiFe2O4) extracted from the Ixora coccinea plant by Taqvi et al. (2022). The detection was achieved based on the electrochemical activity of PCP on the modified NiFe2O4/GCE electrode. The NiFe2O4/GCE sensor showed excellent electrochemical characteristics, with a broad linear range (0.01–90 μM) and LOD of 0.0016 μM for PCP. High sensitivity, specificity, and low cross-reactivity against interferents, such as numerous metal ions and organic substances, were shown. Yet, the performance of the sensor was restricted by small variations in recovery for complex samples (Taqvi et al., 2022). Carbendazim (CBD) is a broad-spectrum carbamate insecticide. Li et al. (2023) reported a dual-mode electrochemical sensor for CBD by using biomass-derived carbon-loaded gold NPs (AuNPs/BC), which were prepared from plant biomass. The AuNPs/BC was immobilized on a glassy carbon electrode (GCE), and then a molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) was prepared from o-aminophenol. The molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) was selectively trapped with CBD, preventing electron transfer in electrochemical reactions. This caused a loss of current response. In DPV mode, a high peak of current appeared due to high affinity between MIP and CBD. Likewise, in EIS, CBD binding enhanced charge transfer resistance (Rct), enabling a measurable effect for CBD quantification. It demonstrated good specificity and low cross-reactivity with interferences. But the performance of the sensor was influenced by slight variations in recovery for complex samples (Qi et al., 2022). Methyl parathion (MP) is a pesticide that is usually responsible for pesticide poisoning among humans. MP damages the nervous system and brain and can be deadly to an organism. An affordable method was used to synthesize porous carbon nanomaterials from expired mung beans (EMBPC) by Han et al. (2024) for the MP detection. The bioderived nanomaterial had a high surface area and conductivity. Zirconium dioxide (ZrO2) NPs were incorporated into EMBPC to form the EMBPC@ZrO2 composite for electrochemical sensing. The composite facilitated electron transfer and effectively adsorbed MP via ZrO2's affinity for phosphate groups. Charge transfer efficiency was improved by porous carbon structure, while DPV measured redox responses in the presence of MP. The EMBPC@ZrO2/GCE sensor showed excellent sensitivity with LOD of 8.77 nM and acceptable recovery (91.95–124.72 %) with less cross-reactivity. Its key drawback was the moderate conductance of ZrO2, which was balanced by the EMBPC structure, for precise detection of pesticides (Han et al., 2024). Apparat from this study, MP was electrochemically sensed by β-cyclodextrin (β-CD)-modified ginkgo nut-derived porous carbon (GNDPC) nanomaterial, prepared from ginkgo nuts using controlled carbonization. The 3D honeycomb-like porous GNDPC was incorporated into a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) to form an electrochemical sensor for MP. The β-CD improved the molecular recognition and adsorption ability, allowing for highly sensitive determination of MP at an LOD of 4.52 nM. Detection was specifically based on the nitro (-NO₂) groups found in MP. These groups were converted to hydroxylamine (-NHOH) in the electrochemical reaction, and then oxidized back to nitroso (-NO) groups. This redox cycle led to a sharp increase in the current, allowing sensitive detection of the pesticide. The sensor showed high specificity and low cross-reactivity with usual interferences, with high recovery rates (95.8–100.1 %) in fruit juices. The synthesis complexity, however, may restrict large-scale applications (Zhao et al., 2023). Imidacloprid is a neonicotinoids class pesticide, has adverse effect on organism even at trace level. Since walnut shells are a suitable source for the carbon nanomaterials synthesis, Devi et al. (2023) hydrothermally synthesized Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) from walnut shells (WS) to detect imidacloprid. These spherical and amorphous nanomaterials, 2–3 nm in diameter, displayed optical behavior, such as fluorescence, which was excitation-wavelength-dependent. Fluorescence "turn-on" detection of imidacloprid by WS-CQDs relied on interaction between surface functional groups of CQDs (-OH, -COOH, -NH2) and the nitro and amine groups of imidacloprid. A ground-state complex was formed, resulting in decreased CQD agglomeration and increased fluorescence via hydrogen bonding and proton exchange, allowing sensitive and selective pesticide detection. The assay showed an LOD of 7.58 μM, with little cross-reactivity, assuring specificity (Fig. 3C). Apart from showing bioimaging and anti-inflammatory promise, a drawback was the decline in the fluorescence lifetime at greater pesticide concentrations that impacted detection efficiency in such cases (Devi et al., 2023) . Glyphosate is widely referred to as a nonselective herbicide that inhibits plant shikimic pathway. This negatively affects the ecosystem owing to its wide specificity. Yang et al. (2023) designed a peroxidase nanozyme-based sensor for glyphosate detection and removal. A bioinspired ZrO2/MnFe2O4/FeZr-MOF nanomaterial was synthesized from naturally hollow Cynanchum seed fiber as a bio-template through impregnation, solvothermal assembly, and calcination. The generated hierarchical micromotors had strong peroxidase-like activity and self-movement owing to MnFe2O4-catalyzed H2O2 decomposition. The detection process relied on peroxidase-like activity inhibition by glyphosate. The ZrO₂/MnFe₂O₄/FeZr-MOF micromotors catalyzed TMB oxidation with H₂O₂ for colorimetric detection. Glyphosate selectively interacted with Zr–O clusters, which scavenged ˙OH radicals and inhibited oxidation, enabling quantification of glyphosate through changes in color intensity. The assay reached an LOD of 0.05 mg/mL under neutral conditions. Zr-O clusters facilitated specific adsorption with high affinity (313.5 mg/g), low cross-reactivity, and fast removal of pollutants. Nevertheless, the detection system was not very selective towards glyphosate when structurally related organophosphorus compounds were present in the sample (Yang et al., 2023).

Food contaminants are toxic compounds inadvertently found in food, such as heavy metals, mycotoxins, and pathogens, that present severe health hazards. Identification of such contaminants is critical for food safety and public health. Plant biomass-based nanomaterials provide a green and cost-effective method for the detection of food contaminants (Ullah and Lim, 2022).

Fluoride ions are contaminants for both food and water. Consumption of these ions imparts health risks to living organisms. CQDs, synthesized from plant biomass waste, are promising nanomaterials for contaminant detection due to their strong fluorescence, high surface functionalization, and excellent biocompatibility. Biomass sources such as sugarcane bagasse, garlic peels, and taro peels were used to synthesize CQDs via ultrasonic-assisted wet-chemical oxidation using hydrogen peroxide. Taro peel-derived CQDs (T-CQDs) exhibited the best fluorescence quantum yield and were utilized by Boruah et al. (2020) to fabricate a T-CQDs/Eu³ ⁺ nanoprobe (Fig. 3D). This nanoprobe detected fluoride ions via fluorescence quenching and regeneration mechanisms, achieving high sensitivity and specificity with minimal cross-reactivity. The detection method involved the fabrication of a T-CQDs/Eu³ ⁺ nanoprobe, where fluorescence quenching occurred upon Eu³ ⁺ binding, followed by fluorescence regeneration upon fluoride ion addition. Sensitivity and specificity were high, with minimal cross-reactivity (Boruah et al., 2020). Cd+2 heavy metals are food contaminants that enters the food chain through water, soil and air. Keerthana et al., (2023) developed a fluorescence sensor for the detection of Cd²⁺ using carbon CQDs derived from water amaranth leaves via a hydrothermal method. The detection method utilized a dual-emission ratiometric fluorescence nanosensor synthesized by functionalizing CQDs with pyrene carboxaldehyde. Fluorescence quenching and intensity shifts were observed upon Cd²⁺ addition, attributed to FRET mechanism. Fluorescence changes enabled selective and sensitive detection in water samples under optimized conditions. The sensor demonstrated a linear detection range of 0–70 µM, a detection limit of 15 nM, and high stability under varied conditions. Although highly sensitive and selective, the nanosensor required longer reaction times than some existing methods, limiting its rapid applicability in certain settings (Keerthana et al., 2023). Hg²⁺ ions are toxic heavy metals that get accumulates in organisms through the food chain and pose serious health risk. For the detection of Hg²⁺. Kaur et al., (2022) pyrolyzed Syzygium cumini leaves to prepare CQDs and silver nanoparticle-modified CQDs (Ag-CQDs). CQDs showed bright green photoluminescence, and Ag-CQDs showed selective colorimetric sensing of Hg²⁺ due to ligand-metal complexation and nanoparticle aggregation. The detection process entailed the use of Ag-CQDs as a colorimetric sensor. On exposure to Hg²⁺ ions, the Ag-CQDs displayed a color change from brownish yellow to colorless upon aggregation of NPs. This was concomitant with a notable drop in the absorption spectra at 423 nm, as followed by UV-Vis spectroscopy. The sensor displayed excellent sensitivity and selectivity towards Hg²⁺, with no significant interference from other metal ions, and hence facilitated effective detection even at low concentrations. The assay showed high specificity and sensitivity within pH 4–10, with negligible cross-reactivity against other metal ions. However, the long-term stability of Ag-CQDs and pH dependency remains a limitation in this assay (Kaur et al., 2022). In another study, Singh et al. (2019) reported a fluorescence-based sensor for the detection of Hg(II) and Cr(VI) ions. A green chemistry approach was applied for the one-step hydrothermal synthesis of nitrogen and phosphorus dual-doped CDs (A-NPCDs) using the biomass of Dunaliella salina (a halophilic microalga). The detection method was based on fluorescence quenching, where the synthesized A-NPCDs were exposed to Hg(II) and Cr(VI) ions. Fluorescence intensity was reduced due to electron transfer and inner filter effect, with the decrease in fluorescence being measured to determine the concentration of the toxic metal ions. The method showed a high sensitivity of 0.018 μM detection limit and selectivity for Hg(II) and Cr(VI), with minimal cross-reactivity to other metal ions. However, specificity can be an issue in complex sample containing other homologous ions (Singh et al., 2019). Sudan I is an artificial dye found in packaged food. This works as a food contaminant as it possesses carcinogenic and mutagenic effects. Cai et al. (2019) synthesized Au–Ag core–shell and alloy NPs using xylan, extracted from plant sources, which acted as a reducing and stabilizing agent. The NPs were fabricated via a seed-mediated method, where xylan prevented oxidation and aggregation while creating hot spots between NPs. The detection mechanism was based on surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS), where the optimized Au@Ag NPs, with enhanced stability, showed high sensitivity and specificity for detecting 4-mercaptobenzoic acid and Sudan I, with limits of detection of 1 nM and 0.126 ppm, respectively. The main limitation of this assay was its performance in environments with potential high oxidation exposure (Cai et al., 2019).

Because iron is an essential nutrient required for a variety of biological processes, Fe2+ ions are not considered dietary contaminants. High concentrations of iron ions in food or water, however, lead to deterioration of quality, e.g., oxidation of fats, and to health risks like oxidative stress and gastrointestinal disorders. George et al. (2024) reported a fluorescence-based sensor to detect of Fe3+ ions. Zero-dimensional CDs (CCDs) were synthesized from the biomass of chayote seeds with a one-pot microwave-irradiated carbonization route. The synthesized CCDs possessed excellent fluorescence characters with an average diameter of 3.3 nm and a quantum yield of 9.56 %. The CCDs were utilized as probes to detect Fe3+ ions via fluorescence quenching. The fluorescence was inhibited by Fe3+ ions through coordination with oxygen-functional groups. The quenching of the fluorescence intensity was measured to quantify the concentration of Fe3+. The CCDs were sensitive and specific towards Fe3+, with low cross-reactivity and an LOD of 4.5 µM. Nevertheless, their use can be restricted by the availability of competing metal ions in intricate environmental samples which is a limitation of this study. (George et al., 2024). Borax, which is a compound of boron, is utilized industrially as a preservative or cleaner. Borax pollutes food through toxicity and interrupting metabolic processes. Zhao et al. (2023) reported a fluorometric assay based on CQDs for the detection of borax. CQDs were synthesized from discarded tobacco leaves using a green, one-step hydrothermal method. A fluorescence-based detection method was developed for borax, which exhibited high sensitivity with a detection limit of 8.0 µM. The fluorescence intensity increased linearly with borax concentration in the range of 0.025–5.0 mM. The method demonstrated excellent specificity with minimal cross-reactivity with other substances (Zhao et al., 2023). Roxarsone (ROX) is an arsenic-based drug used in poultry production. Nikhil et al. (2022) reported the electrochemical detection of roxarsone (ROX) using a two-dimensional activated carbon (2D-AC) modified glassy carbon electrode (GCE). The 2D-AC was synthesized from Desmostachya bipinnata, an Indian plant, via chemical activation with potassium hydroxide (KOH). The material's meso/microporous structure and high surface area enhance its electrocatalytic activity. ROX was reduced at a specific potential, generating a measurable current and this current was recorded using DPV. The NPs enhanced electron transfer, improving the sensitivity and electrochemical response for ROX detection. The LOD was achieved to be 1.5 nM with minimal cross-reactivity. However, in the mixture of structurally similar molecules, specificity can be a limitation of this study (Nikhil et al., 2022).

These nanomaterials are biocompatible and affordable equivalent to chemically synthesized nanomaterials in the area of environmental monitoring and healthcare. By eliminating harmful chemicals and utilizing plant biomolecules like proteins and polyphenols improves the biocompatibility and functional versatility of these materials. These materials exhibit high sensitivity and selectivity, making them effective for detecting heavy metals, pesticides, pathogens, and biomarkers. However, variability in plant extracts and challenges in scaling up can limit their consistency and widespread use. In contrast, chemically synthesized nanomaterials provide superior control over size, shape, and surface functionalization, enabling high-performance biosensing with enhanced detection limits and specificity(Vernekar et al., 2021, Kumar et al., 2019). Despite their precision, they are energy-intensive, costly, and environmentally hazardous. While chemically synthesized options are better suited for high-performance and durable devices (Won et al., 2013, Agrawal et al., 2013), plant-derived nanomaterials are ideal for sustainable and disposable sensing applications (Baran et al., 2025). A hybrid approach combining the strengths of both could address limitations and enhance their biosensing potential.

Plant-derived nanomaterials, synthesized using plant extracts, demonstrate unique physiochemical properties and versatile medical applications. These applications include drug delivery, regenerative medicines, cancer treatment, and antimicrobial therapies. For example, Zinc-oxide NPs (ZnO-NPs) synthesized from Deverra toruosa extract have exhibited significant activity against human colon adenocarcinoma (Caco-2) and human lung adenocarcinoma (A549) cell lines by reducing tumor growth while sparing healthy cells (Selim et al., 2020). Another example is AgNPs synthesized from Azardirachta indica (neem), which are effectively used in delivering anticancer drugs like doxorubicin, and promise wound healing properties (Dutt et al., 2023).

Edible plant-derived NPs from ginger have been employed to deliver chemotherapeutic agents like doxorubicin more effectively, protecting it from premature degradation and ensuring site-targeted release (Zhang et al., 2016). Plant-derived NPs derived from Curcuma longa (turmeric) have shown promise in treating inflammatory and neurodegenerative disorders by exhibiting anti-inflammatory effects by inhibiting NF-κB signalling pathways (Memarzia et al., 2021). Regenerative medicine is another field in which PDNMs are being studied. For example, Aloe vera derivedNPs, loaded with growth factors, have shown potential in wound healing by promoting angiogenesis, enhancing fibroblast migration, and accelerating re-epithelialization. Therefore, it is useful for treating chronic wounds and burns (Adel Alawadi et al., 2024). Similarly, cellulose nanofibers are also being explored for tissue engineering scaffolds in bone and cartilage tissues, providing a conducive environment for cell proliferation and differentiation (Iravani and Varma, 2022).

The global rise in antimicrobial resistance (AMR) has prompted researchers to explore plant-derived NPs as natural alternatives to traditional antibiotics. AgNPs synthesized from Azadirachta indica have shown potent antimicrobial activity against multidrug-resistant pathogens, including Escherichia coli, Staphylococcus aureus, and Candida albicans. These nanomaterials also exhibit strong antioxidant properties, making them useful in managing oxidative stress-related diseases. Carbon NPs derived from Syzygium cumini (black plum) have been shown to possess excellent free radical scavenging activity by neutralizing ROS (Pricilla et al., 2024). Plant-based nanomaterials are the new frontier in modern therapeutics, offering a sustainable and versatile platform for drug delivery, regenerative medicine, antimicrobial treatments, and more. By tapping the bioactive potential of plants, scientists have devised NPs that better deliver therapy with many of its worst challenges: antimicrobial resistance and oxidative stress. Despite these challenges, such as toxicity and scalability, ongoing developments in nanotechnology and biochemistry promise to unlock the full potential of plant biomass-derived nanomaterials in improving healthcare outcomes.

Plant biomass-derived nanomaterials are a novel class of nanostructures made from naturally renewable resources such as plant leftovers, algae, microbes, and animal-derived compounds. Currently, plant biomass-derived nanomaterials have sparked interest over traditional components because of their sustainability, biocompatibility, and unique characteristics (Raja et al., 2019). Their distinctive physiochemical features, such as large surface area, superior mechanical performance, and low toxicity, make them suitable candidates for biomedical applications, especially bioimaging (Harish et al., 2022). Bioimaging is a non-invasive visualization technology used to study and visualize real-time internal structures and physiological processes in living cells and organisms. Multiple non-invasive optical imaging techniques, such as Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI), computed tomography (CT), positron emission tomography (PET), Ultrasound and Single Photon Emission CT (SPE-CT), are now shifting to the nanostructures because of their customizable optical, magnetic, and acoustic capabilities (Harish et al., 2022). NPs increase the sensitivity and specificity of diagnostic tests and are frequently designed to target specific tissues or molecular markers (Fang et al., 2019, Sun et al., 2008). For example, fluorescent NPs are better than traditional molecular probes in terms of sensitivity and therefore, could be alternatively used in bioimaging (Kim et al., 2018). The superparamagnetic properties of magnetic NPs, such as iron-oxide NPs, have also recently gained popularity owing to their role in image-guided therapy. Plant biomass-derived nanomaterials have shown great promise in resolving the toxicity, poor resolution, and scalability associated with traditional imaging agents. Another advantage is the absence or reduction of long-term biological and environmental issues compared to conventional nanomaterials. Their versatility and multifunctionality have been highlighted in several studies, underscoring their applications in vascular imaging, theragnostics, and cancer diagnostics ( Fig. 4).

Fluorescent imaging makes it possible to visualize biological activities at cellular/molecular levels using the luminous qualities of materials (Qureshi et al., 2024). Biomass-derived nanomaterials, particularly carbon-based nanomaterials have emerged as promising candidate in fluorescent imaging due to their sustainability, biocompatibility and versatile functionalities. Biomass sources such as wood pulp, xylan, banana leaves etc. have been utilized to generate CDs and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) (Korram et al., 2023, Dkhar et al., 2024, Wang et al., 2020). Several recent studies have highlighted their role in biomedical imaging (Dkhar et al., 2024, Kumar et al., 2024b, Mahato et al., 2020). Singh et.al provided a comprehensive review of CDs, focusing on their synthesis, fluorescence properties, and drug delivery applications (Singh et al., 2024). Their excitation-dependent fluorescence arises due to their quantum confinement effects and surface states (Li et al., 2017). CDs synthesized from banana leaves have also demonstrated high fluorescence quantum yields and photostability therefore making them a suitable candidate (Catronas et al., 2023). Another study concluded that on conjugation with targeting ligands, such as heteroatoms (sulfur, nitrogen or phosphorus) these nanomaterials may allow for highly targeted imaging of malignant tissues (Nair et al., 2020). In a study, CQDs derived from oyster shells, CPKS and citric acid sources etc. exhibited fluorescence after UV-light exposure. These probes can modulate inflammation along with the potential for organelles bioimaging and diagnosis studies for the immune system (Kumar et al., 2024a). In another study, microalgae-derived luminescent materials were used for fluorescence visualization. Similarly, Mimosa pudica leaves’ derived CDs also showed fluorescent emission abilities along with minimal toxicity (Dhanush et al., 2024).
Altogether, these findings demonstrate the potential role of different biomass-derived NPs in fluorescent imaging.

Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) is a diagnostic tool that uses magnetic fields and radio waves to generate high-resolution images of internal structures. Although it is widely used however, certain limitations are still present in terms of the quality of contrast agents which enhance the image quality by altering the relaxation times of water protons in tissues (Caspani et al., 2020). Traditionally, gadolinium-based contrasting agents, were used possessing certain limitations such as nephrotoxicity, environmental persistence, and high costs. To overcome these, Magnetic nanomaterials are gaining attention due to their ability to be functionalized with other biomolecules such as gadolinium or iron oxides, low-cost, biocompatibility, as well as tuneable optical, magnetic and electrical properties which results in enhanced image quality (Lan et al., 2023). Various biomass-derived nanomaterials have been employed for their use in MRI. For example, rice-husk derived silica NPs integrated with gadolinium are used to produce cost-effective and efficient MRI contrast agents (Araichimani et al., 2020). Apart from gadolinium, plant biomass-derived nanomaterials can incorporate superparamagnetic iron oxide NPs (SPIONs) for strong magnetic properties. These materials act as T2 or T1 contrast agents enhancing the signal to contrast ratio in MRI scans (Das et al., 2019). Another promising candidate in MRI and fluorescence bioimaging is lignin-based NPs synthesized using agricultural and paper and pulp industry byproducts. These NPs have dual magnetic and photoluminescent properties (Arcentales et al., 2023), therefore, acting as a cost effective as well as functional alternative to conventional agents. Carbon Dots, have also gained massive popularity in cancer diagnostics by overcoming the traditional challenges associated with therapy such as cytotoxicity, multidrug resistance, lack of specificity, etc (Lan et al., 2023). These CDs are being synthesized by waste like banana peels, rose petals or lignocellulosic biomass as discussed above. Lignocellulosic nanomaterials derived from plant wastes show high antioxidant properties along with photoluminescence characteristics, making them ideal candidates for imaging for disease diagnostics (Arcentales et al., 2023). In addition to carbon, rare-earth elements like terbium, when integrated into silica NPs showed enhanced specificity and sensitivity of MRI scans by providing a strong signal to noise ratio (Sharma, 2024).
These findings underscore the importance of plant biomass-derived nanomaterials in Magnetic Resonance Imaging for disease diagnostics.

Photoacoustic imaging (PAI) is a hybrid imaging modality of optical imaging along with ultrasound (Wang and Yao, 2024). It uses ultrasound waves to create high–resolution tissue images, therefore it can be used for biomedical applications like vascular imaging, tissue detection and functional monitoring of organs (Wang and Yao, 2024). Traditionally used contrasting agents such as organic dyes and metallic NPs, show major limitations of biocompatibility, eco-friendliness and environmental sustainability. Thus, the use of biomass-derived NPs in PAI has brought a more ecologically friendly and cost-effective way to improve imaging capabilities. These nanomaterials, derived from natural and renewable resources, have unique optical, thermal, and structural characteristics that make them ideal contrast agents in PAI. CDs made from agricultural waste, fruits, and other organic precursors display good optical absorption in the near-infrared (NIR) range, which is an important property for effective photoacoustic contrast agents. Doping with heteroatoms or functionalizing with organic or inorganic groups can improve the efficiency of light absorption and photothermal conversion in these materials (Redondo-Fernandez et al., 2023). For example, hollow carbon nanospheres derived from okara showed dual benefits of diagnosis and treatment by h demonstrating significant potential in imaging guided photothermal therapy (Weng et al., 2019). Neem-extract derived Gold NPs have also proven effective in the imaging of tumor vasculature using PAI (Varna et al., 2018).

Computed tomography (CT) imaging involves differential attenuation of X-rays through tissues and contrasting agents. Traditionally used contrasting agents have low stability and potential toxicity as well. Therefore, there biomass-derived NPs provide a sustainable and safer alternative. In CT imaging, plant biomass-derived nanomaterials combined with heavy atoms like iodine, gold or bismuth are used to offer high X-ray attenuation i.e. clearer and detailed imaging. These dopings ensure contrast enhancements along with overcoming the limitations of synthetic agents. It is shown that bismuth doped biomass-derived carbon nanomaterials are a promising CT contrast agents, due to their high atomic number, biocompatible and biodegradable nature (Gavin et al., 2023). Another study demonstrated the use of iodine-functionalized carbon nanodots synthesized from biomass, showing excellent CT contrast enhancement along with prolonged circulation times therefore overcoming the limitations like rapid renal clearance and short imaging windows (Jeong et al., 2022). In a study by Giuri et al. demonstrated the synthesis of CNPs using waste ashes which exhibited excellent X-ray attenuation therefore, highlighting the potential as suitable candidates in CT imaging (Striani et al., 2021). Spirullina biomass synthesized bismuth NPs (Hosseini et al., 2020) and silica NPs from plant materials (Prabha et al., 2021) also showed low toxicity along with high attenuation. These nanomaterials can be hybridized with other compounds for more versatility. Etemad et al. investigated cellulose based nanocrystals as templates for CO2 foam stabilization in CT imaging (Etemad et al., 2022).

One of the most exciting developments in this field is the use of plant biomass-derived nanomaterials in multimodal imaging and theragnostic. Theragnostic involves the integration of therapeutics and diagnostics into a single platform. By combining MRI along with fluorescence, PET or Ultrasounds, versatile diagnostic tools can be created which can be capable of visualizing and monitoring multiple biological processes simultaneously. Different use of plant biomass derived nanomaterials are depicted in Fig. 5.

Carbon-based nanomaterials, such as CDs and hollow carbon nanospheres, are common examples. CDs produced from biomass display outstanding photoluminescent characteristics, making them useful in bioimaging and fluorescence-guided treatment (Jayaprakash et al., 2024). Gedda et al. described the potential applications of CDs in multimodal imaging like fluorescence and photoacoustic imaging for deep tissue visualization (Gedda et al., 2021). Biomass-based NPs, coupled with fluorescent dyes (Cy5) or particular targeting molecules can be used for MRI and fluorescence imaging concurrently (Zhang et al., 2014). Belemkar et al., explored green nanotechnology which enables gold NPs for theragnostic applications in cancer, utilizing its bifunctionalized surfaces for high-contrast images (Gao et al., 2021). Similarly, Functionalized magnetic CDs were investigated as fluorescence/MRI-guided cancer treatment. Zhong et al. stated their capacity to function as theragnostic platforms for tumor diagnostics and treatment (Zhong et al., 2023). Although, these NPs represent sustainable and innovative alternative in bioimaging, however, they possess several challenges like scalability, reproducibility and regulatory approvals which if overcome can pave the way for a comprehensive diagnostic tool.

Nanomaterials synthesized from plant biomass have a great deal of promise for use in disease diagnostics, pathogen detection, pesticide detection, food contaminant, environmental monitoring and many more. However, a number of obstacles prevent their synthesis and broad applications in biosensing, therapeutics and bioimaging. One of the primary barriers is aggregation of NPs because of strong interparticle interactions, which occurs when higher extract concentrations of biomass is used for the synthesis. Their homogeneity and dispersion are impacted, which eventually restricts their usefulness. Furthermore, these nanomaterials frequently show decreased stability at high temperatures and pH levels, which limits their applicability in a variety of environmental and biological contexts (Terea et al., 2024, Kumari et al., 2020).

The variation in nanoparticle yield, size, and shape is another important drawback. The shapes and sizes of NPs vary widely which could change their functional characteristics. Reproducibility might be impacted by inconsistent size and distribution which is essential for large-scale applications. Furthermore, differences in the phytochemical composition of plant extracts frequently affect yield, which is batch-dependent. It can be challenging to accomplish consistent production and scalability because of those variations (Gokulakrishnan et al., 2023, Khane et al., 2022). Scalability is still a significant barrier to these NPs' industrial use. The synthesis process frequently takes several days to complete, and batch variability make large-scale production even more challenging. The lack of thorough research on their long-term stability in industrial settings raises questions about their prospects for application. For synthesis processes to be viable for large-scale applications, the extraction and synthesis process must be optimized to increase their stability and reproducibility (Sabouri et al., 2024, Ameen et al., 2023).

In biosensing applications, plant biomass-derived nanomaterials face several challenges, including sensor performance issues and specificity limitations. Modified electrodes, such as plant-derived ZnO nanoparticle-modified GCE, suffer from surface fouling during repeated measurements, which can degrade their long-term performance (Manikandan et al., 2024). Additionally, many biosensing assays rely on pH optimization, limiting their adaptability in varying sample conditions. Cross-reactivity with similar analyte in complex samples is another issue that affects the specificity of biosensors (Ashikbayeva et al., 2023). Moreover, certain immunosensors require extended incubation times for antibody immobilization and antigen interaction, making them less suitable for rapid detection scenarios (Zhou et al., 2022).

A number of limitations also impact bioimaging applications. For example, detection efficiency is decreased due to a decrease in fluorescence lifespan. Aggregation of NPs can affect signal strength, producing erratic imaging outcomes. These nanomaterials' specificity and performance are further impacted by sensitivity problems that occur when they interact with homologous or competing metal ions in complicated biological or environmental samples. The broad application of plant-based nanomaterials is also constrained by practical and environmental constraints. Certain carbon-based nanomaterials have reduced surface areas because of their amorphous carbon content, and their performance may be affected under conditions with high oxidation exposure (Palisoc et al., 2020). Furthermore, accurate control and optimization of these nanomaterials are hampered by our poor understanding of the molecular transformation mechanisms involved in the production process (Gedda et al., 2023). To improve their stability, scalability, and effectiveness in biosensing, bioimaging, and other biomedical applications, these constraints must be addressed through additional study, optimization, and standardization.

Because of unique properties like environmentally friendly synthesis, biocompatibility, and tuneable features, nanomaterials derived from plants have enormous potential in the advancement of biosensing and bioimaging applications. By utilizing plant biomass extracts or phytochemicals as natural reducing and stabilizing agents, these nanomaterials can be synthesized with nontoxic chemicals and high energy inputs.

For the detection of a wide range of analytes, including pollutants, contaminants, pathogens, and biomarkers, these nanomaterials exhibit significant potential in biosensing. For example, plant-derived metal and metal oxide NPs have been effectively used in colorimetric, electrochemical, magnetic, and fluorescence-based biosensors. These biosensors enable quick and inexpensive diagnoses, which is particularly useful in resource-constrained settings. The inherent versatility of plant-derived nanomaterials also allows them to be functionalized with biomolecules like enzymes, antibodies, or aptamers, which increases their use in multiplexed sensing systems.

These nanomaterials are used as contrast agents for bioimaging methods like fluorescence, magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), and computed tomography (CT). The phytochemical-based natural capping agents increase their in vivo stability, toxicity, and biocompatibility. All these qualities are of critical importance for imaging in living systems, where safety and long-term circulation are paramount. In addition, due to their optically and magnetically tenable properties, plant-based nanomaterials are utilizable for multimode imaging, through which they offer a holistic understanding of cellular as well as molecular-level biological processes.

Along with their advantages, reproducibility, scalability, and fluctuation in the plant biomass content are also challenges. The solution to these issues requires standardizing synthesis protocols and bringing advanced analysis techniques on board. In addition, further research needs to be conducted to assess the long-term stability and biocompatibility of such nanomaterials in complex biological environments.
